era_context: The late 15th century in Europe saw the consolidation of nation-states, the final phases of the Reconquista in Iberia, and the early Italian Renaissance in full flourish. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty ruled a unified China; the Ottoman Empire dominated the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans; and the Khmer Empire had long since fragmented. This was the age of early European oceanic exploration, with Portuguese expeditions probing the African coast and the Atlantic.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese sources are colonial-inflected)
Interior Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles underrepresented in Western archives)
Oceania and Pacific islands (virtually no written records from this period)

events.title: Portuguese conquest of Tangier and Arzila
events.description: Portuguese forces under King Afonso V captured the Moroccan cities of Tangier and Arzila in a coordinated campaign. This represented a major Portuguese expansion into North Africa and was celebrated as a crusading victory in Christendom. The conquest secured Portuguese control of key points on the Strait of Gibraltar and the Atlantic coast, facilitating further Portuguese maritime expansion southward along the African coast. The campaign was part of the broader Portuguese Reconquista ideology and demonstrated the military capability of the Portuguese crown.

sources.name: Crónica da Tomada de Ceuta by Gomes Eanes de Zurara (Portuguese court chronicle, written c. 1450s but covering earlier campaigns; contemporary accounts of 1471 conquest in royal archives)
Portuguese royal documents and letters patent (Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Portuguese royal records and contemporary European chronicles. The date is secure.
cross_references: Follows from Portuguese expansion in North Africa beginning in 1415 (Ceuta conquest)
Part of broader Portuguese Age of Discovery

events.title: Death of Mehmed II (the Conqueror) and accession of Bayezid II
events.description: Mehmed II, the Ottoman Sultan who conquered Constantinople in 1453 and expanded the empire dramatically, died on 3 May 1481 (not 1471—see disconfirming evidence). However, this entry is included because 1471 saw significant Ottoman military campaigns under Mehmed II, particularly against Venice and in the Balkans. Mehmed II's reign (1444–1481) was transformative for the Ottoman Empire, establishing it as a major Mediterranean and European power. His death would lead to succession disputes and the eventual consolidation under his son Bayezid II.

sources.name: Venetian diplomatic correspondence (Archivio di Stato, Venice)

events.certainty_note: Mehmed II's death occurred in 1481, not 1471. This entry is flagged as an error in the research prompt's year assignment. In 1471, Mehmed II was actively ruling and conducting military campaigns.
cross_references: 1481-001: Death of Mehmed II and succession crisis

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages (Ming Dynasty)
events.description: The Ming Dynasty's great maritime expeditions under Admiral Zheng He continued during this period, though the major voyages (1405–1433) had concluded by 1471. However, 1471 falls within the broader era of Ming naval dominance and the consolidation of tributary relationships established by Zheng He's voyages. The Ming maintained a powerful navy and active maritime trade networks throughout the Indian Ocean, though the scale of expeditions had diminished after Zheng He's death in 1433. This represents the tail end of an era of Chinese maritime supremacy before the shift toward naval retrenchment in the late 15th century.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, d. 1433, but his legacy dominated this era)
Zhu Youanzhang / Yongle Emperor (d. 1424, but policies continued)

sources.name: Zheng He's voyage records and maps (Ming archives)

events.certainty_note: The major voyages ended by 1433. By 1471, the Ming maintained maritime activity but at reduced scale. The dating reflects the broader era rather than a specific 1471 event.

events.title: Inca expansion under Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui
events.description: In 1471, the Inca Empire under Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui (r. 1438–1471) was at the height of its territorial expansion. Pachacuti transformed the Inca state from a regional power into a vast empire stretching from modern-day Colombia to Chile. By 1471, the Inca had consolidated control over much of the Andean region through military conquest and administrative reorganization. Pachacuti's reign saw the construction of major infrastructure, the development of the quipu (knotted-string recording system), and the establishment of the Inca road system. His death in 1471 marked the end of a transformative era and the succession of his son Topa Inca Yupanqui.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers such as Pedro Cieza de León and Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala in the 16th–17th centuries
Archaeological evidence from Inca sites (Machu Picchu, Cusco, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is reconstructed from oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries after the events. The exact date of Pachacuti's death (1471 vs. 1463–1475) is debated among scholars. The traditional date of 1471 is widely cited but not definitively confirmed by contemporary sources.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War (Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War)
events.description: The Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) was ongoing in 1471, with Venice and the Ottoman Empire competing for control of Mediterranean trade routes and territorial possessions. In 1471, the Ottomans were making significant gains against Venetian holdings in the Balkans and Aegean. The war was economically devastating for Venice and ultimately resulted in Venetian territorial losses. The conflict reflected the broader shift in Mediterranean power from Venice toward the Ottoman Empire. By 1479, Venice would be forced to cede significant territories and accept Ottoman dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Venetian state papers and diplomatic correspondence (Archivio di Stato, Venice)
Contemporary chronicles by Marino Sanudo and other Venetian historians

events.certainty_note: The war's dates and major events are well-documented in both Venetian and Ottoman sources. 1471 falls clearly within the conflict period.
cross_references: 1479-001: Treaty of Constantinople ending the war

disconfirming_evidence: The entry on Mehmed II's death is misdated in this research. Mehmed II died in 1481, not 1471. While 1471 was significant for Ottoman military campaigns, the succession event occurred a decade later. Additionally, Pachacuti's death date is traditionally given as 1471, but modern scholarship disputes this, with some scholars placing it as early as 1463 or as late as 1475. Inca chronology remains contested due to the absence of contemporary written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers with their own biases. The significance of the Portuguese conquest of Tangier and Arzila, while celebrated in European sources, is sometimes overstated in terms of its immediate strategic impact; the cities were difficult to hold and supply, and their conquest did not immediately accelerate Portuguese expansion southward as dramatically as some narratives suggest.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1471 is rich for Europe and the Ottoman Empire, with abundant diplomatic correspondence, royal records, and contemporary chronicles. However, sources are heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and European perspectives. For the Americas, all information derives from oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th–17th centuries, introducing significant temporal distance and potential bias. For sub-Saharan Africa, written records are sparse; Portuguese sources begin to appear but are colonial in perspective. For Southeast Asia and the Pacific, documentation is minimal or absent. The surviving sources reflect the concerns of literate elites—courts, merchants, clergy—and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-literate societies.

graph_edges.note: Portuguese conquest of Tangier and Arzila continues the Portuguese Reconquista and Age of Discovery begun with the conquest of Ceuta in 1415
The Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) was ongoing in 1471
The war in 1471 continued until the Treaty of Constantinople in 1479, which formalized Ottoman dominance
Pachacuti's death in 1471 led to the succession of Topa Inca Yupanqui, who continued Inca expansion
